content.text: What Review Gates Are and Why GenAI Needs Them

A review gate is a mandatory checkpoint at a defined phase transition in a project lifecycle. The project cannot proceed past the gate until specific, pre-agreed criteria have been demonstrated. Gates are different from reviews: a review is advisory, a gate is blocking. Review gates exist because the cost of fixing problems grows by roughly an order of magnitude at each phase transition — a model selection mistake caught at Gate 2 (Architecture) costs a week; caught at Gate 4 (Production Readiness) it costs months.

GenAI systems require a different gate structure than conventional software because their failure modes are different. A conventional system either works or throws an error. A GenAI system can work correctly in technical terms — no exceptions, within latency budget — while producing outputs that are hallucinated, biased, or unsafe. Conventional gates check for working code. GenAI gates must additionally check for accurate, safe, compliant, and cost-controlled behaviour.

attrs.alt: Five review gates across the GenAI project lifecycle

content.text: Gate 1 occurs at the end of inception, before significant engineering effort begins. Its purpose is to validate that the problem is real, that AI is the right solution, and that the organisation has the data, regulatory clearance, and sponsorship to proceed.
User research summary showing the problem exists and AI can address it better than alternatives
Explicit justification why rule-based or conventional ML is insufficient; documented in project charter
Sample of training/retrieval data reviewed; volume, quality, and licensing confirmed
Identified applicable regulations (GDPR, sector-specific rules); DPO has been consulted
Back-of-envelope cost estimate at target scale; within budget ceiling
Named executive sponsor has approved the project charter and budget

Gate 1 does not require a working prototype. It requires a written project charter that addresses each criterion. Teams that skip Gate 1 commonly discover at Gate 3 (PoC Exit) that the problem was not well-defined, the data does not exist in usable form, or the regulatory exposure was not understood — at which point months of engineering work must be discarded.

Gate 2 occurs after the solution architecture has been designed and the key ADRs have been written, but before implementation begins. It is the primary DRB gate — the point at which the Design Review Board formally approves the set of architecture decisions that will govern the build.
ADRs for model selection, architecture pattern, and eval framework accepted by DRB
PII data flow documented from ingestion to storage to deletion; reviewed by DPO
All third-party data processors (model provider, vector DB, embedding API) covered by signed DPA
STRIDE or equivalent completed; prompt injection, PII leakage, and abuse scenarios documented
Latency, accuracy, cost, and availability targets defined and approved by product
Eval dataset design, scorer choice, and CI gate specification approved

Gate 2 is the highest-leverage gate in the lifecycle. Architecture decisions made here constrain every subsequent phase. A DRB condition raised at Gate 2 and resolved before implementation begins costs hours. The same issue discovered during load testing at Gate 4 costs weeks.

Gate 3 occurs at the transition from proof-of-concept to production build. The PoC has demonstrated that the approach is technically feasible; Gate 3 verifies that it is safe, compliant, and worth building to production quality.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
from enum import Enum

class GateStatus(str, Enum):
    PASS = "pass"
    FAIL = "fail"
    WAIVED = "waived"  # with documented reason and approver

@dataclass
class Gate3Criterion:
    criterion_id: str
    description: str
    is_blocking: bool
    status: GateStatus = GateStatus.FAIL
    evidence: Optional[str] = None
    waiver_reason: Optional[str] = None
    waiver_approver: Optional[str] = None

@dataclass
class Gate3Review:
    project: str
    review_date: str
    criteria: list[Gate3Criterion] = field(default_factory=list)

    def blocking_failures(self) -> list[Gate3Criterion]:
        return [
            c for c in self.criteria
            if c.is_blocking and c.status == GateStatus.FAIL
        ]

    def can_proceed(self) -> bool:
        return len(self.blocking_failures()) == 0

    def summary(self) -> str:
        total = len(self.criteria)
        passed = sum(1 for c in self.criteria if c.status == GateStatus.PASS)
        waived = sum(1 for c in self.criteria if c.status == GateStatus.WAIVED)
        failed_blocking = len(self.blocking_failures())
        status = "GO" if self.can_proceed() else "NO-GO"
        return (
            f"Gate 3 [{status}]: {passed}/{total} pass, {waived} waived, "
            f"{failed_blocking} blocking failures"
        )

# FNOL claim chatbot Gate 3
gate3 = Gate3Review(
    project="fnol-claim-chatbot",
    review_date="2025-01-15",
    criteria=[
        Gate3Criterion("ACC-01", "Classification F1 >= 0.87 on held-out eval set", True,
                       GateStatus.PASS, "F1 = 0.891 on 420-item eval set (2025-01-12 run)"),
        Gate3Criterion("SAF-01", "PII red-team: 0 leaks on 10k synthetic interactions", True,
                       GateStatus.PASS, "Red-team report v1.2 — 0 PII leaks detected"),
        Gate3Criterion("SAF-02", "Prompt injection suite: 0 critical failures", True,
                       GateStatus.PASS, "87/87 injection scenarios blocked"),
        Gate3Criterion("COST-01", "Cost per request < £0.015 at production token volumes", True,
                       GateStatus.PASS, "Measured £0.011/req on 1000-request sample"),
        Gate3Criterion("DATA-01", "Eval dataset in version control; baseline scores committed", True,
                       GateStatus.PASS, "Committed to fnol-evals repo; CI pipeline live"),
        Gate3Criterion("OPS-01", "Rollback procedure documented and tested", True,
                       GateStatus.FAIL,
                       evidence=None),  # BLOCKING: not yet done
        Gate3Criterion("OPS-02", "Runbook first draft complete", False,
                       GateStatus.WAIVED,
                       waiver_reason="Full runbook due at Gate 4; draft sufficient for PoC exit",
                       waiver_approver="Alice Chen (Principal Architect)"),
    ]
)

print(gate3.summary())
# Gate 3 [NO-GO]: 5/7 pass, 1 waived, 1 blocking failures

for f in gate3.blocking_failures():
    print(f"  BLOCKING: {f.criterion_id} — {f.description}")
# BLOCKING: OPS-01 — Rollback procedure documented and tested

The Gate 3 example above shows the mechanism in operation: five criteria pass, one is waived with documented approval, and one blocking failure prevents progression. The team must complete the rollback procedure before Gate 3 can be re-reviewed. The gate is not a hurdle to clear by arguing; it is a checklist where each item is either evidenced or explicitly waived.

Gate 4 occurs immediately before production launch. All systems are built to production quality; the gate confirms that operations are ready, compliance is complete, and the system can be safely launched and monitored.
Load test at 2× expected peak passed
Load test report: P95 latency, error rate, auto-scaling verified
All SLO alerts live and routed to on-call
Screenshot of dashboard; Ops lead confirms alert receipt test
Full runbook written and tabletop exercise completed
Runbook URL; tabletop exercise sign-off from Ops Lead
Guardrail red-team at production configuration passed
Red-team report signed off by Security Lead
DPO and Legal final sign-off received
Signed approval email or ticket; DPIA filed in compliance register
Rollback procedure tested in staging environment
Rollback test report; successful rollback demonstrated
Monthly cost ceiling enforced; billing alerts active
Screenshot of billing alert configuration; ceiling amount approved
Ops team has completed shadow on-call period criteria
Shadow on-call schedule confirmed; Ops Lead briefed

Gate 4 is the last opportunity to prevent a launch that will produce an incident. The gate should be reviewed by the full DRB or at minimum the Chair + Platform Lead + Security Lead. An incomplete Gate 4 checklist is not a minor risk — it is a documented prediction of the type of incident the team will experience in the first 90 days.

Gate 5 occurs 30 days after launch. It is the final gate — the transition from 'newly launched with PoC team support' to 'steady-state operation owned by Ops'. It is the gate most commonly skipped, because by day 30 the PoC team has moved on and no one organises the review. Skipping it leaves the Ops team in a perpetual state of informal support from people who are no longer accountable.
All SLOs met for 30 consecutive days
Grafana dashboard shows 30-day history; no sustained SLO breach
Actual monthly cost within agreed ceiling
Billing statement for first full month reviewed by Cost Controller
Quality score P50 >= baseline; no regression trend
30-day quality score time series; no downward trend in last 14 days
Zero P1 incidents; P2 incidents have root causes and mitigations
Incident log reviewed; all P2s have mitigation ADRs or runbook updates
Ops team handled last 3 alerts without PoC team involvement
Shadow on-call log; PoC team co-sign that they were not consulted
All production ADRs have review dates in the calendar
ADR index reviewed; review dates in team calendar

Gate 5 produces a steady-state handoff document — a brief (one-page) record that the system is operating normally, the Ops team owns it, and the PoC team's ongoing availability is no longer required. This document closes the PoC engagement formally and prevents informal 'just a quick question' support that persists for months.

A gate that always passes is not a gate — it is a ceremony. The value of a gate is proportional to how often it catches something that would otherwise become a production problem. When a gate fails, the process must handle it without becoming a demoralising bureaucracy.
Proceed to next phase; document passing evidence in ADR or project record
Document failures; assign owners and due dates; proceed with plan to resolve before next gate
Stop; resolve failures; re-present only the failed criteria to gate reviewer (no full board re-review)
Stop; hold a retrospective to understand why so many criteria failed; revise approach before re-gate
Author submits written waiver request with rationale and compensating control; Chair approves or escalates to DRB
Same session if Chair can decide; or next DRB session

A gate waiver is not a shortcut — it is an explicit decision to accept a known gap with a documented compensating control and a named owner. The waiver must be as well-documented as a failed criterion that was resolved: who approved it, why, what compensates for it, and when it will be revisited.

Criteria from earlier gates often impose constraints on later ones. If a Gate 2 architecture decision assumed a specific vector database, Gate 4 load testing must validate that database at production scale. If a Gate 1 regulatory scope assessment identified GDPR obligations, Gate 3 must verify PII handling and Gate 4 must show DPO sign-off. Tracking these dependencies prevents teams from treating each gate as an isolated checklist.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class GateDependency:
    from_gate: int
    from_criterion: str
    to_gate: int
    to_criterion: str
    dependency_type: str  # "constrains", "evidence-required-by", "assumption-tested-at"
    note: str

# FNOL chatbot — cross-gate dependency map
FNOL_DEPENDENCIES: list[GateDependency] = [
    GateDependency(
        from_gate=1, from_criterion="Regulatory scope: GDPR identified",
        to_gate=2, to_criterion="DPA coverage confirmed",
        dependency_type="constrains",
        note="Gate 1 GDPR identification means Gate 2 must verify DPA for all processors",
    ),
    GateDependency(
        from_gate=2, from_criterion="ADR-001: Azure OpenAI West Europe",
        to_gate=3, to_criterion="PII red-team: 0 leaks on provider endpoint",
        dependency_type="constrains",
        note="Red-team must target the specific provider and region approved in ADR-001",
    ),
    GateDependency(
        from_gate=2, from_criterion="ADR-003: RAG with 4-hour index freshness SLA",
        to_gate=4, to_criterion="Load test at 2x peak passed",
        dependency_type="assumption-tested-at",
        note="Load test must include vector index at production corpus size to validate ADR-003 SLA",
    ),
    GateDependency(
        from_gate=3, from_criterion="Eval dataset committed to version control",
        to_gate=4, to_criterion="Guardrail red-team at production configuration",
        dependency_type="evidence-required-by",
        note="Gate 4 red-team must use the same eval set committed at Gate 3 to ensure comparability",
    ),
    GateDependency(
        from_gate=4, from_criterion="Full runbook written and tabletop exercise completed",
        to_gate=5, to_criterion="Ops team handled last 3 alerts without PoC team involvement",
        dependency_type="constrains",
        note="Gate 5 Ops independence assumes Gate 4 runbook was complete and Ops team trained on it",
    ),
]

def check_dependency_chain(resolved_criteria: set[str]) -> list[str]:
    broken = []
    for dep in FNOL_DEPENDENCIES:
        if dep.from_criterion in resolved_criteria:
            if dep.to_criterion not in resolved_criteria:
                broken.append(
                    f"Gate {dep.to_gate} criterion '{dep.to_criterion}' may be incomplete: "
                    f"depends on Gate {dep.from_gate} '{dep.from_criterion}' ({dep.dependency_type})"
                )
    return broken

# Example: check before Gate 4 review
resolved = {
    "Regulatory scope: GDPR identified",
    "ADR-001: Azure OpenAI West Europe",
    "ADR-003: RAG with 4-hour index freshness SLA",
    "Eval dataset committed to version control",
    # "Full runbook written and tabletop exercise completed"  # <- not yet done
}
issues = check_dependency_chain(resolved)
for issue in issues:
    print(f"  WARNING: {issue}")

Each gate requires a different set of participants. Inviting the full DRB to a Gate 1 (Problem Framing) review wastes time; having only the Principal Architect at a Gate 4 (Production Readiness) review misses critical operational and compliance sign-offs.

'Required' means the gate cannot proceed without this person's participation or explicit written approval. 'Consulted' means their input was sought during preparation but their presence at the gate session is not mandatory. 'Informed' means they receive the gate outcome but do not participate in the review.
For smaller teams or faster-moving projects, the five-gate model can be implemented with minimal overhead using a single checklist file per project.

# docs/gates/fnol-chatbot-gates.yaml
project: fnol-claim-chatbot
last_updated: 2025-01-20

gate_1_problem_framing:
  status: PASSED
  date: 2024-10-15
  evidence: docs/project-charter-v1.0.md

gate_2_architecture:
  status: PASSED
  date: 2024-12-07
  evidence:
    - docs/adr/ADR-001-model-provider.md (Accepted)
    - docs/adr/ADR-003-rag-architecture.md (Accepted)
    - docs/drb/minutes/2024-12-07-gate2.md

gate_3_poc_exit:
  status: PASSED
  date: 2025-01-16
  evidence: docs/gates/gate3-exit-report-2025-01-16.md
  waivers:
    - criterion: OPS-02 (full runbook)
      reason: Draft sufficient for PoC exit; full runbook due at Gate 4
      approved_by: Alice Chen
      due: 2025-02-01

gate_4_production_readiness:
  status: IN_PROGRESS
  target_date: 2025-02-01
  outstanding:
    - "Runbook tabletop exercise (Ops Lead sign-off pending)"
    - "DPO final sign-off (scheduled 2025-01-28)"

gate_5_30_day_review:
  status: NOT_STARTED
  target_date: 2025-03-15

Five review gates structure the GenAI project lifecycle: Problem Framing (Gate 1) before engineering begins; Architecture (Gate 2) after ADRs are written; PoC Exit (Gate 3) before production build; Production Readiness (Gate 4) before launch; and 30-Day Review (Gate 5) for steady-state handoff. Each gate has defined, evidence-based criteria that are either passed, failed with documented remediation, or waived with written approval.

The gates derive their value from being blocking. A gate that is always waived or always passed is a ceremony without effect. The waiver process — explicit approval, documented compensating control, named owner — preserves the gate's integrity when circumstances genuinely require proceeding before a criterion is fully met, while maintaining the audit trail that makes the governance system credible.
